column4: Does the prompt reflect the assessed skill?
column6: Is there a single correct answer?
column7: Is there parallelism among the alternatives?
column10: Do the alternatives include elements not present in the stimulus?
column11: Is the item adequately aligned with the assessed skill?
column12: Could the item be used in an assessment with little or no modification?
column13: Observation

Justification D is wrong because it presupposes a double answer key by using “No apenas” to refer to the first cause. The command includes one extra element: “mark the correct alternative.” There is a lack of syntactic parallelism among the alternatives.
Repeated item.

The item would need to be substantially reformulated. Its main problem is the way the command proposes the task, which makes it unsuitable for the grade level. There are also problems in the construction of the alternatives (parallelism—the answer key has the longest statement, making it more attractive).

The item would need to be substantially reformulated. Its main problem is the way the command proposes the task, which makes it unsuitable for the grade level. There are also problems in the construction of the alternatives (lack of syntactic parallelism).

The item does not assess the skill, which involves recognizing the cause of an action based on the most common causal conjunctions. The command asks for the consequence. The way the statement is framed, by excerpting a sentence from the text, is also inadequate.
The item does not assess the described skill, nor any other skill in the Portuguese language reference frameworks. In this sense, it is not an item that can be used for large-scale assessment.
Because the command focuses on a passage, the distractors are plausible, since they refer to the text as a whole. The answer key has a longer statement, making it more attractive (parallelism).
The item assesses a complex metalinguistic competence: identifying what is usual or not in the language. The item is unsuitable for any grade level.

Double answer key (B and D) — The command is partially adequate: only at the end. It presents metalinguistic information that makes it too complex for the grade level assessed (“because indicates a causal relation”). By excerpting the cause-and-effect statement from the text, the command makes the task easier, focusing it on considering a sentence and not the text.
Alternative C is plausible because the information is in the text — the conjunction “visto que” is not very common, therefore unsuitable for the grade level assessed.
Although the answer key is correct, it has a parallelism problem in relation to the other alternatives. It is attractive. The command is partially adequate: only at the end.

By excerpting the cause-and-effect statement from the text, the command makes the task easier, focusing it on considering a sentence and not the text. The answer key shows syntactic parallelism, which makes it attractive.
There is a lack of syntactic parallelism among the alternatives, and alternative D is plausible because it contradicts the command (calm/nervous).

There is a lack of syntactic parallelism among the alternatives (three alternatives have an omitted subject — they refer to Sofia). In some justifications, the claim that there are events prior to Sofia’s happiness is coherent, since all of them are prior (the cause is always earlier).
There is a lack of syntactic parallelism among the alternatives, especially in the answer key, which becomes very attractive.
Command too complex for the grade level. More focused on the wording than on the text.

The command is too complex and unsuitable for assessing the skill at this grade level. Parallelism — in the construction of the alternatives, the answer key differs from the distractors (by using a negative form), making it very attractive. Negative forms can be used in alternatives.
Parallelism — the answer key has the longest statement, standing out from the others and becoming attractive.
Error in the wording of justification B.
The command is focused more on analyzing the wording than on reading the text, making the task easier.
Inadequate command, making the task too complex for the grade level.
The command is inadequate because it excerpts the sentence from the text. It is also complex because it uses the term “indicate” (too complex for the schooling stage).
The command is inadequate: too complex for the schooling stage. Justification C is incoherent.
This item assesses a skill for more advanced stages, for example lower secondary education, given the lexical complexity of the item as a whole.

The command reflects the skill only partially in its final part: what was the reason Carlos did not go to the party. The rest hinders understanding of the task, since the connector because marks a different causal relation from the one requested in the last part of the command. The justification for the answer key is wrong, because the connector because marks the causal relation requested and not the one that precedes it. The requested causal relation is not marked.
Lack of parallelism — option A (the answer key) stands out. Option D is a plausible distractor.
The command assesses another skill related to the idea of time and not causality, which invalidates the item.

The command does not reflect the skill because the word “pelas” is a common conjunction. It is the contraction of the preposition “por” with the article “as,” which, although it signals a causal relation, does not do so explicitly as common conjunctions such as because, since, and as. The reader may possibly infer the idea of cause from the relation between the statements. This is the use of a grammatical category not mentioned in the skill description.
The answer key is correct in content, but the explanation in parentheses is a paraphrase of the command wording, making the item very easy. The item could be used with modifications.

The cause-and-effect relation requested by the command is marked by a common conjunction, as explained above. However, the item could be used with a modification to the support text: replacing “pelas” with a more common causal conjunction.

The cause-and-effect relation requested by the command is marked by a common conjunction, as explained above. However, the item could be used with a modification to the support text: replacing “pelas” with a more common causal conjunction.

The item has a double answer key, because alternative C is highly plausible and is the cause of Mariana’s happiness, although not announced by the conjunction “pois.” The item command, by highlighting the passage from the text, contributes to the creation of this ambiguity.
Lack of parallelism among the alternatives, especially in the answer key. Justification A is correct because it also expresses a cause, but not the main one, which justifies the request.
Alternative B is plausible — it contradicts the question asked.
There is an error in the wording of the answer key regarding tense usage (ensaiei). Alternative A is plausible. Justification D is valid.
The item command highlights a passage asking for the consequence of a fact. However, the relation is marked, so it does correspond to the described skill, which invalidates the item.

It is worth noting a particular feature of this item. Although the causal relation is marked by the conjunction “because,” it involves an inferential relation that links the “prize” to the “short story book,” which makes the task more complex.
Distractors B and D are plausible because they negate statements from the text. Justifications B and D are adequate.

The item command does not reflect the skill because it asks for recognition of the “event” and not the “cause” or “consequence.” Therefore, the justifications are not supported. Alternatives A and C are plausible.

The answer key is incorrect and the item does not assess the skill. The command refers to a fragment of the text in which the causal relation is not explicit, nor marked by a common conjunction or by the contiguity of statements in a causal relation. This relation must be inferred, and the item assesses inference production.
The item does not assess the described skill, but rather the ability to locate explicit information.
Alternative D is not very plausible.
Alternative D is not very plausible.
Alternative D is not very plausible.
The command reflects the skill, but its format makes the task too easy. Distractors B and C are not very plausible.
The command does not reflect the skill because the word “pelas” is a common conjunction, as explained above. This makes the item invalid.
The command does not reflect the skill because the word “pelas” is a common conjunction, as explained above. This makes the item invalid.
The command reflects the skill, but its format makes the task too easy. The distractors are not very plausible.

The item has problems with the distractors. They are plausible, especially considering their relation to the command and the item’s answer key. Distractors B and C, for example, refer to facts related to Patrick, while the command mentions the award given to students.
The support text is level 3. Double answer key: B and C.

The command does not reflect the skill, because it asks for recognition of a common conditional conjunction (a metalinguistic knowledge item). The skill concerns recognizing the conditional relation, or the condition of an action/fact, marked by a conjunction.
